title: 有翼之暗
summary:
McAtar provides a detailed analysis of the motives behind locked-room mysteries, categorizing them into six types: hindering prosecution, vanity/games, professionalism, accidental occurrence, and others. He dismisses several categories for the current case, noting that the locked room was not created to prevent prosecution since the murders of Aoyagi and Shizuma were sufficient for charges. He identifies the most likely current explanation as the fifth category—vanity or a game—but then introduces a seventh concept: "situation construction."

McAtar argues that the decapitation and mutilation of the victims were not the primary goals, but rather "decorations" designed to create a specific environment. This "situation construction" was intended to make it appear natural for the investigator, Mutsu, to stay at Kurokawa Castle to investigate the Ito-Hitomi murder case. By creating an incredible crime, the killer ensured Mutsu's involvement, thereby allowing the real culprit, Wakizaki, to maintain control over the investigation.

Regarding the first double homicide, McAtar asserts that Woodmatsu acted with an accomplice. He suggests that the threatening letter Woodmatsu showed to Hasegawa was a forgery designed to provide a motive for his visit to the Inari house. Furthermore, McAtar explains the locked room in "Hell's Gate" as a simple deception: the accomplice locked the door from the outside using Ito's key. When the body was discovered, Woodmatsu—who was the first to find the key—placed it in the left hand of the deceased, Hama. This explains why the right-handed Hama held the key in his left hand, as his right hand was pinned beneath his body.
